title: Electronics Flip-and-Dispute: Resale-Oriented Friendly Fraud on Premium Devices
narrative: Cardholder purchases high-resale electronics ($800-$2,500 smartphones, laptops, gaming consoles) with genuine credentials, lists items on secondary markets (eBay, OfferUp, Facebook Marketplace) within 24-48 hours, completes resale, then initiates chargeback 45-70 days post-purchase claiming non-receipt or defective goods. Pattern emerged strongly in Q4 2022 with iPhone 14 Pro launches and persists across premium SKUs. Typical sequence: legitimate card_txn_count_7d of 1-2, single high-value electronics purchase ($1,200 median TransactionAmt), delivery to verified addr1, then dispute filing coinciding with resale platform payout timelines. Fraudster retains resale proceeds while recovering original payment. Often uses personal P_emaildomain (gmail.com, yahoo.com) matching cardholder name, clean historical velocity, and daytime purchase hours (txn_hour 10-16) to appear legitimate until dispute phase reveals intent.
indicators: TransactionAmt between $800-$2,500 on ProductCD='W' (electronics) with card1_txn_count_30d < 5
Single high-value transaction representing card1_amt_zscore > 2.5 compared to 90-day history
Delivery to established addr1 with dist1 < 50km from billing, clean AVS match
P_emaildomain matches common free providers (gmail.com, yahoo.com, outlook.com) with 6+ month account age
Purchase during business hours (txn_hour between 9-17, is_night=0)
Chargeback filed 45-75 days post-transaction (near dispute deadline window)
ProductCD category shows high resale liquidity: smartphones, laptops, consoles, cameras
summary: A customer buys a single high-value electronic item ($800–$2,500) using their own legitimate card, has it shipped to their verified address, then files a chargeback 45–75 days later claiming they never received it or it was defective—right after the item has been resold on a marketplace.
reasoning: The fraudster exploits chargeback protections to double-dip: they pocket cash from reselling the electronics on eBay or Facebook Marketplace, then recover the original purchase price by disputing the transaction, effectively getting paid twice for the same device.
indicator_explanations: Premium electronics purchase on card with minimal recent transaction history
Transaction amount wildly exceeds customer's typical spending pattern baseline
Item ships to verified home address near billing location with clean address verification
Legitimate-looking free email account that's been active for months, not disposable
Purchase made during normal shopping hours, not typical fraud time windows
Dispute filed strategically near maximum chargeback deadline after product received
Product type chosen specifically for easy cash resale on secondary markets
